citation: Teramoto, M., Cushman, D. M., Cross, C. L., Curtiss, H. M., & Willick, S. E. (2017). Game schedules and rate of concussions in the National Football League. The Orthopaedic Journal of Sports Medicine, 5(11), 2325967117740862.
abstract: This descriptive epidemiological study examined whether unconventional NFL game schedules—including Thursday games (3 days of rest) and overseas games—are associated with an increased rate of concussions. Using PBS Frontline Concussion Watch data, 582 concussions were analyzed across the 2012–2015 NFL regular seasons. Chi-square goodness-of-fit tests and logistic regression were used to examine the relationship between days of rest, game location, bye week timing, and concussion rates. No significant association was found between concussion rates and days of rest, game location, or bye week timing. Significantly more concussions occurred in the second half of the season (56.2% vs. 43.8%, P < .01).
tldr: Contrary to public concern, Thursday Night Football and overseas games do not increase NFL players' concussion risk. The only notable finding was a higher concussion rate in the second half of the season, suggesting cumulative fatigue may be a more meaningful risk factor than schedule format. Team medical staffs should heighten vigilance later in the season rather than specifically around short-rest games.
methods: Descriptive epidemiological study using publicly available data from PBS Frontline Concussion Watch and Pro-Football-Reference.com. Analyzed 582 concussions across 2012–2015 NFL regular seasons. Chi-square goodness-of-fit tests compared observed concussion distributions by days of rest (3, 5–7, 9–14 days) and game location (home, away, overseas) to expected distributions based on actual game scheduling proportions. Two-way contingency table analysis and logistic regression assessed repeat concussion risk, controlling for player position.
findings: No significant association between concussion rate and days of rest (P = .17), game location (P = .23), or bye week timing by team or opponent (P > .05). Game schedules were not predictive of repeat concussions (P > .05). Significantly more concussions occurred in the second half of the season than the first half (56.2% vs. 43.8%, P < .01). Non-significant trends included ~15 more concussions than expected after 9–14 days of rest and 39 more in away games than home games.
limitations: Reliance on a publicly available database rather than the NFL Injury Surveillance System may have resulted in missed or unverified concussions. Small sample size reduced statistical power, particularly for Thursday and overseas games. Individual player factors (condition, skill level, prior concussion history) were not controlled. Observational design prevents causal inference. Concussion underreporting in football is a known issue.
practicalImplications: Current NFL game schedules—including Thursday Night Football and international games—do not appear to independently elevate concussion risk. Scheduling policy changes related to player safety should focus on other risk factors. Medical and training staffs may consider increased concussion monitoring during the second half of the season when cumulative fatigue is highest.
athleteDev: For player development contexts, findings suggest that schedule density (short rest) is less influential on head injury risk than cumulative seasonal load. Programs should incorporate fatigue management across the full season rather than focusing primarily on short-week preparations.
rtp: The absence of a significant relationship between days of rest and repeat concussions provides some reassurance when managing return-to-play timelines around short rest weeks. However, clinicians should remain vigilant for players returning late in the season when overall concussion rates are elevated.

citation: Khalil, L. S., Lindsay-Rivera, K. G., Abbas, M. J., Shah, S., Tandron, M., Ferris, A., & Okoroha, K. R. (2021). Game utilization and performance following RTP from ACL reconstruction does not influence a subsequent second ACL injury in National Football League players. Arthroscopy, Sports Medicine, and Rehabilitation, 3(5), e1377–e1385.
abstract: This retrospective case-control study examined whether in-game utilization or performance following primary ACL reconstruction differs between NFL players who subsequently sustain a second ACL injury (retear group, n=13) and those who do not (tear group, n=32). Players who underwent ACL reconstruction between 2013 and 2017 were identified from public sources. Game utilization (games played, games started, snap counts) and season approximate value (SAV) were compared across three seasons post-RTP relative to the pre-injury baseline season. No significant differences were found between groups in utilization or performance metrics at any time point.
tldr: How much an NFL player plays after ACL reconstruction does not predict whether they will suffer a second ACL tear. Both groups showed similar declines in game usage and performance after RTP. The one meaningful predictor of return to starting lineup was draft pick position, suggesting organizational investment—not individual workload—drives post-ACL playing time decisions. Clinicians cannot use in-game utilization as a risk stratification tool for second ACL injury.
methods: Retrospective case-control study of NFL ACL injuries from 2012/2013 to 2017/2018 seasons. Players were identified via publicly available sources and classified into 'tear' (primary ACL only, n=32) and 'retear' (subsequent ipsilateral or contralateral ACL injury, n=13) groups. Game utilization statistics (games played, games started, snap counts) and season approximate value (SAV) were collected from pro-football-reference.com, ESPN.com, and NFL.com. Data were analyzed for the season prior to injury (baseline) and three seasons following RTP. Statistical comparisons used independent t-tests, Wilcoxon rank sum tests, chi-square tests, Fisher's exact tests, and repeated-measures analysis. Relative percentages used pre-injury baseline as reference.
findings: No significant differences in games played, games started, snap counts, or SAV between tear and retear groups across all three post-RTP seasons (P > .05 for all comparisons). Both groups demonstrated similar decreases in all utilization metrics relative to pre-injury baseline. Tear players averaged >100% of baseline for most metrics; retear players generally failed to reach 100% but differences were non-significant. Draft pick position was positively correlated with the relative percentage of games started in the first post-RTP season (r = .554, P = .017). Return to play rate was 73.3% following primary ACL reconstruction.
limitations: Retrospective design with data from public internet sources introduces potential for incomplete or inaccurate injury classification. Cannot be generalized to non-NFL athlete populations or other levels of play. Concomitant injuries (meniscal, cartilage), surgical technique, graft choice, and rehabilitation protocols were not standardized or controlled. Sample size was too small to independently analyze ipsilateral vs. contralateral reinjuries. No power analysis conducted. Players with pre-NFL ACL history were excluded, limiting generalizability.
practicalImplications: Game utilization after ACL reconstruction cannot serve as a modifiable risk factor or risk screening tool for second ACL injury in NFL players. Organizational decisions about returning high-draft-pick players to starting roles appear to be independent of reinjury risk. Clinicians and performance staff should not restrict or modulate game utilization based on reinjury prevention rationale alone. Other biomechanical, neuromuscular, and psychological factors should be prioritized in return-to-sport screening.
athleteDev: For player development after ACL reconstruction, draft position—a proxy for organizational investment and perceived talent—was the primary factor predicting return to a starting role. Development programs should focus on optimizing neuromuscular readiness and psychological readiness for RTP rather than managing game minutes as a reinjury prevention strategy, since load management did not differentiate those who re-tore from those who did not.
rtp: Game utilization and performance metrics following primary ACL reconstruction are not predictive of a subsequent second ACL tear. Return-to-play protocols should not use post-RTP snap counts or games started as risk stratification criteria. The similar decline in performance metrics in both tear and retear groups suggests that standard RTP decision-making should focus on objective functional and strength criteria rather than graduated game exposure as injury prevention.

citation: Roberts, S. S. H., Urwin, C. S., Regan, J., Bowe, S. J., & Warmington, S. A. (2025). Game-day temperatures are predictive of National Football League game outcomes when teams from different climates compete against each other. Temperature. Advance online publication.
abstract: This observational study examined whether game-day air temperature recorded at NFL stadia predicts game outcomes when teams from cool climates (>39° N latitude, North teams, n=17) compete against teams from warm climates (<39° N, South teams, n=15). Data from 1,011 regular-season and playoff games across eight NFL seasons (2017–2025) were extracted from official NFL Gamebooks. Mixed effects logistic regression and mixed effects models tested whether temperature associated with win probability and point differential, respectively, while controlling for season year, home team ID, and end-season rank differential.
tldr: When NFL teams from cold-climate cities (north of 39°N) play against warm-climate teams, their win probability and point differential both decline as game-day temperature rises—even after accounting for home field advantage. Each 1°C increase in temperature reduces a northern team's odds of winning by roughly 2–3%. The effect is strongest for games played in the South, suggesting that players and staff from warmer regions may have behavioral or psychological adaptations to heat that confer a competitive advantage.
methods: Historical game data from eight NFL seasons (2017–2025) collected from NFL Gamebooks. Teams categorized as North (>39° N, n=17) or South (<39° N, n=15) based on home stadium latitude. Analyzed 1,011 games with reported game-day temperature. Mixed effects logistic regression modeled North team win probability per 1°C temperature increase; mixed effects models examined North team point differential per 1°C increase. Models run for all games combined and separately by game location (North vs. South). Random effects included season year, home team ID, and end-season rank differential. Humidity and game location added as covariates in follow-up models.
findings: North team win probability declined significantly as game-day temperature increased (OR = 0.974, 95% CI [0.951, 0.997], p = .026), representing a 2–3% reduction per 1°C rise. This association held when controlling for humidity and game location. North team point differential was negatively associated with temperature (β = -0.175, 95% CI [-0.286, -0.063], p = .002), but this became non-significant when game location was included as a covariate. By game location, North team point differential was significantly associated with temperature for games played in South locations (β = -0.152, p = .001) but not North locations (p = .318). North teams won 59% of games played in the North but only 49% of games played in the South.
limitations: The 39°N latitude cutoff for North/South classification is somewhat arbitrary. Data limited to eight seasons from official NFL Gamebooks. Difficult to fully control for inherent differences in team quality across games, though random effects were included. Home time-zone and circadian misalignment were not controlled. Temperature recording methodology at NFL stadiums is not publicly standardized. Observational design cannot establish causal mechanisms.
practicalImplications: Teams based in cold-climate cities face a measurable competitive disadvantage when playing in warmer environments. Preparation strategies for warm-weather away games should include proactive heat acclimatization protocols, hydration planning (including potential IV hyperhydration), and in-game cooling strategies. Staff familiar with warmer conditions may have optimized behavioral routines that contribute to their teams' advantage. Northern teams should treat warm-weather road trips as a specific performance challenge requiring targeted environmental preparation.
athleteDev: NFL players from northern franchises competing in warm environments may benefit from heat exposure training during the preseason or before warm-weather road trips. Psychological preparation—reducing perceptual discomfort and reinforcing confidence in warm conditions—may also be underutilized. Teams should audit their thermal management protocols for warm-weather games as a competitive edge opportunity.
rtp: While not directly an RTP study, findings are relevant for scheduling return-to-competition after injury: players returning to play in unfamiliar climate conditions (particularly warm environments after cold-climate training) may face additional physiological and cognitive demands. Rehabilitation and reconditioning programs should consider environmental acclimatization as part of full RTP readiness, especially for travel to warm-weather venues.

citation: Foster, G., O'Reilly, N., Devereux, J. B., & Shundi, M. (2023). General manager and head coach exits in the NBA and the NFL. Sport, Business and Management: An International Journal. Advance online publication.
abstract: This study investigated why head coaches and general managers (GMs) exit their positions in the NBA and NFL over a 30-year period. Using 891 GM and coach annual observations from NBA clubs (1988–2018) and 949 observations from NFL clubs (1987–2017), three hypotheses grounded in agency theory, upper-echelon theory, and institutional logic theory were examined. Methods included logit analysis for exit/retention modeling and latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) topic modeling of qualitative press quotations. Findings show that simultaneous GM and coach exits are strongly correlated, win-loss record is more predictive of coach exits than GM exits, and past organizational culture of turnover strongly predicts future exits.
tldr: In professional football and basketball, coaches are fired primarily based on losing records, while GMs are dismissed for a more complex mix of reasons beyond wins and losses. When one key executive leaves, the other is significantly more likely to follow—the 'cleaning house' effect is statistically real. Perhaps most practically, a club's past culture of firing coaches and GMs is the strongest predictor of future exits, meaning organizational instability tends to perpetuate itself and should be a key consideration for any executive evaluating a job offer.
methods: Quantitative and qualitative analysis using 30 years of data. Logit analysis modeled the probability of GM and coach exit (binary: start vs. not start following season with same club) as a function of win-loss record, playoff progression, simultaneous exit of the other role, and historical turnover frequency. Separate models run for NBA, NFL, and pooled samples. Qualitative analysis used Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) topic modeling on a database of press quotations compiled from Sports Business Daily, Bleacher Report, and ESPN.com for all GM and coach exit events. Two topics emerged from LDA: 'Win-Loss Record' and 'Beyond Win-Loss Record.'
findings: Hypothesis 1 (partially supported): Win-loss record significantly predicts coach exits in both NBA and NFL, but is much weaker as a predictor for GM exits. Hypothesis 2 (supported): Simultaneous exits of GMs and coaches are significantly correlated—a coach exit significantly increases the probability of a GM exit and vice versa (the 'cleaning house' effect; P < .001 across models). Hypothesis 3 (supported): Past organizational turnover frequency is strongly negatively associated with retention—clubs with high historical exit rates continue to exhibit high exit rates (P < .001). NFL GM mean tenure was 5.27 seasons; NFL coach mean tenure was 4.14 seasons. Annual exit rates averaged 16.0% for NFL GMs and 21.3% for NFL coaches.
limitations: Club ownership and ownership changes—a likely key driver of GM and coach exits—could not be fully modeled due to data limitations. The exit/retention classification is binary and may not capture nuanced departures (voluntary vs. involuntary). Press quotation databases are sparse for early years of the study period due to limited online content. The 'Beyond Win-Loss' LDA topic is heterogeneous, reflecting the complex and varied nature of non-performance-related GM exits. Causal inference is not possible from this observational design.
practicalImplications: Organizations with a high historical rate of GM and coach turnover are likely to continue that pattern, creating instability that affects player recruitment, team culture, and long-term performance. Executives and coaches evaluating new opportunities should assess an organization's historical turnover culture as a key contractual risk factor. Clubs should consider that cleaning house rarely addresses the root cause (often ownership behavior) and may perpetuate dysfunction. Win-loss record remains the primary lever for head coach retention in both leagues.
athleteDev: Organizational leadership stability is an underappreciated factor in athlete development. Frequent GM and coach turnover disrupts player development pipelines, scouting philosophies, and long-term roster building. For football programs, evaluating the historical turnover culture of an organization before committing long-term is critical for players, agents, and incoming coaches seeking environments conducive to sustained development.
rtp: This study does not directly address return-to-play from injury. However, organizational instability caused by frequent coaching and GM turnover can disrupt sports medicine infrastructure, rehabilitation protocols, and player health management continuity. Stable leadership environments are likely more conducive to consistent, evidence-based RTP decision-making.

citation: de Oliveira, D. M., Silva, A. K. A. C., Macedo, A. G., Fernandes, M. B., & Fernandes, E. V. (2025). Global research trends in sports nutrition and football over the last 20 years (2004–2024). Sports, 13, 365.
abstract: This scientometric review mapped global scientific production on sports nutrition applied to soccer (football) from 2004 to 2024. A database search across Web of Science, PubMed, and Scopus yielded 2,636 documents, of which 526 original articles were included after removing duplicates, reviews, meta-analyses, and out-of-scope studies. Data were analyzed using Bibliometrix and VOSviewer software. The study identified publication trends, leading authors, journals, countries, collaboration networks, and thematic clusters. Annual publication growth rate was 14.6% with a 1,450% increase over the 20-year period. The United Kingdom led in total output and citations, with Nutrients as the top publishing journal.
tldr: Research on sports nutrition applied to soccer has grown dramatically over 20 years, shifting from a primary focus on hydration to increasingly complex topics including low energy availability, polyphenol supplementation, body composition, and recovery strategies. Underexplored areas—oxidative stress markers, beta-alanine supplementation, and low energy availability—represent high-priority gaps for future research. For practitioners, this landscape review highlights that the evidence base for individualized nutritional strategies in football is expanding rapidly, with particular attention warranted on energy deficiency and antioxidant support.
methods: Scientometric analysis of original research articles published from January 2004 to December 2024. Databases searched: Web of Science, PubMed, and Scopus. Boolean search strategy combined nutritional terms (dietary intake, sports nutrition, energy availability, hydration, supplementation, macronutrient/micronutrient intake) with football/soccer terms. After deduplication and eligibility screening, 526 articles were included. Bibliometrix 4.3.2 (R 4.4.2) used for descriptive analyses, production trends, authorship, journal distribution (Bradford's Law), citation analysis, and country production. VOSviewer 1.6.20 used for keyword co-occurrence networks and author/institution collaboration mapping. Conceptual structure mapped via multiple correspondence analysis (MCA).
findings: Annual scientific production grew 1,450% from 2004 (4 articles) to 2024 (62 articles) at a 14.6% annual growth rate. Nutrients was the leading publication source (53 articles, 29.6% of total). Morton J. (n=12) and Maughan R. (n=10) were the most productive authors. Liverpool John Moores University was the primary institutional collaboration hub. United Kingdom led in both volume (245 articles, 46.6%) and citations (1,847). Early research focused on hydration and thermoregulation (2006–2012); later research expanded to macronutrient intake, body composition, and energy balance (2013–2020); most recent trends (2021–2024) emphasize low energy availability and polyphenol supplementation. Co-occurrence network identified underexplored topics including lipid peroxidation, alpha-tocopherol, beta-alanine supplementation, and oxidative stress markers.
limitations: Restricted to three databases; gray literature and non-indexed journals were excluded. The 20-year time frame, while comprehensive, may not capture pre-2004 foundational research. Exclusion criteria removed American football, Australian rules football, rugby, futsal, and adapted football studies. Scientometric methods identify patterns but do not evaluate the quality or methodological rigor of individual studies. Database coverage discrepancies may affect results.
practicalImplications: The field of sports nutrition in soccer is rapidly maturing toward individualized, performance-focused strategies. Practitioners should be aware of the growing evidence base for low energy availability as a risk factor in soccer players, polyphenol supplementation for recovery and oxidative stress management, and beta-alanine for high-intensity anaerobic performance. Hydration and carbohydrate periodization remain foundational, while emerging topics offer opportunities for evidence-based innovation in nutritional protocols.
athleteDev: For football athlete development, the scientometric landscape confirms that nutritional support should be integrated across multiple dimensions: hydration and thermoregulation, carbohydrate and energy availability management, supplementation (polyphenols, vitamin D, beta-alanine), and body composition monitoring via anthropometry. Programs should build nutritional protocols informed by the most cited research clusters and be aware of underexplored areas—particularly low energy availability in younger and female players—that may have meaningful developmental implications.
rtp: The growing research emphasis on recovery nutrition, oxidative stress management, and energy availability in soccer players has direct relevance to return-to-play contexts. Nutritional strategies targeting inflammation, muscle damage (polyphenols, antioxidants), and energy resynthesis (carbohydrates, total energy intake) should be integrated into RTP rehabilitation protocols. Low energy availability—an emerging focus of the field—may compromise recovery and tissue healing, making energy intake monitoring a key component of injury return management.
